access_mechanism_type: formal_procurement (regulatory bottleneck affects all channels — EPS-PBS, MIPRES/non-PBS, and tutela-driven access depend on INVIMA sanitary registration)
procurement_channels: Not directly procurement; INVIMA approval is a precondition for all channels: EPS-PBS, MIPRES/non-PBS, private pharmacy, tutela-mandated procurement.
key_actors: INVIMA (Instituto Nacional de Vigilancia de Medicamentos y Alimentos) — the registration authority; Directorate of Medicines and Biological Products (Dirección de Medicamentos y Productos Biológicos) — operational unit handling registrations; Ministerio de Salud y Protección Social (MINSALUD) — supervising ministry; Technical Committee jointly composed of INVIMA + MINSALUD officials oversees Resolution 2025010547 contingency execution.
formulary_coverage: Not applicable directly — INVIMA registers medicines; PBS formulary inclusion is a separate MINSALUD decision (Resolution 2718/2024). However, INVIMA approval is a prerequisite before any oncology drug (cisplatin, doxorubicin, carboplatin, trastuzumab) can be marketed or reimbursed in Colombia.
judicial_access_volume: [uncertain] — no specific tutela count tied directly to INVIMA backlog; however, ~5,700 of the 14,000+ pending registrations are part of a 'judicial evacuation plan' indicating litigation pressure on backlog clearance.
registration_timeline_generic: Officially 3-6 months; in practice 6-12 months due to backlog. New drugs manufactured abroad and aligned with Colombian pharmacological norms ~80 days. Generics evaluation generally 120-180 business days. Registration valid for 5 years. [uncertain on exact 2025 average post-Resolution 2025010547]
registration_timeline_biologic: [uncertain] — specific 2024-2025 figure not published. Historical Decree 1782 (2014) data: average ~28 months for biologics/biosimilars (2020 reference point). New drugs and biologics: officially 6-12 months, can extend to 18 months under standard process; longer in practice given backlog.
registration_authority: INVIMA (Instituto Nacional de Vigilancia de Medicamentos y Alimentos), legal basis Decree 1290/1994 and subsequent decrees (Decree 677/1995 for medicines; Decree 1782/2014 for biologics). Reports to Ministerio de Salud y Protección Social.
biosimilar_pathway: Decree 1782 of 2014 establishes three pathways: (1) full dossier (expediente completo), (2) comparability pathway (vía de comparabilidad), (3) abbreviated comparability pathway (vía abreviada de comparabilidad). All require quality, safety, efficacy and pharmacovigilance evidence.
registration_backlog: 14,000+ pending sanitary registrations under review by INVIMA's Directorate of Medicines and Biological Products as of April 2025 (2025 INVIMA Procedures Observatory). Approximately 5,700 of these were submitted before November 2023 and are included in a judicial evacuation plan. Between November 2023 and September 23, 2025, INVIMA reported resolving 23,229 of 26,049 pending procedures.
reform_status: enacted — Resolution 2025010547 effective March 19, 2025, introduces a six-month Contingency Plan. Establishes Technical Committee (INVIMA + MINSALUD) to oversee execution. InvimÁgil digital platform launched May 8, 2025 (initial phase: food and alcoholic beverages). Reform actively implementing through 2025-2026.
currency_mechanism: Floating COP — no formal currency controls. Importers exposed to FX volatility but no rationing mechanism.
pharma_import_cost_impact: [uncertain] — not specifically quantified in INVIMA-related sources; not a direct factor in registration backlog.
financial_mechanism: Not applicable directly — INVIMA charges registration fees; downstream financing flows through EPS-UPC, MIPRES recobro, presupuestos máximos.
payer_debt_crisis: Not directly applicable to INVIMA, but downstream effect: backlog delays new generics/biosimilars market entry, reducing competitive pressure that could relieve pricing burden on EPS-IPS pharmaceutical debt (>COP 24 trillion total, COP 4.2 trillion pharma).
shortage_rate: [uncertain] — no specific shortage rate attributable to backlog alone; INVIMA reports 8 medicines in shortage state, 5 at risk, 181 under monitoring as of Dec 2024.
shortage_causes: INVIMA registration backlog contributes structurally to shortage by delaying new product entry, manufacturer changes, and import authorizations. Backlog itself caused by understaffing, complex dossier review, and accumulated arrears from prior years. Resolution 2025010547 prioritizes plant inspections, import authorizations, and manufacturer changes.
shortage_monitoring_system: INVIMA centralized 'Desabastecimientos' module since May 1, 2018. Resolution 2025010547 introduces enhanced procedural oversight; separately, MINSALUD developing SISCAATS (Sistema de Información para la Centralización de Alertas de Abastecimiento de Tecnologías en Salud) with mandatory weekly reporting for manufacturers, importers, wholesalers.
oncology_specific_shortage_events: [uncertain] — INVIMA's Dec 2024 list of 8 shortages did not specifically name cisplatin/doxorubicin/carboplatin/trastuzumab. However, backlog is documented to delay new oncology generics/biosimilars market entry.
source_citation: 1) DDReg Pharma, 'New INVIMA Plan Aims to Streamline Regulatory Processes in Colombia,' 2025, https://resource.ddregpharma.com/insights/new-invima-plan-aims-to-streamline-regulatory-processes/. 2) U.S. Department of Commerce / trade.gov, 'Colombia Healthcare Pharmaceutical Regulatory Acceleration' and 'Colombia Healthcare Sanitary Registration Backlog,' 2025. 3) Freyr Solutions, 'Navigating INVIMA's New Contingency Plan,' 2025. 4) Navlin Daily, 'Colombia's INVIMA Unveils Plan to Address Regulatory Backlog,' 2025. 5) INVIMA, Resolution 2025010547 of 2025 (effective March 19, 2025).
source_type_granular: government_primary (INVIMA Resolution 2025010547; trade.gov U.S. government market intelligence) + legal_regulatory_tracker (DDReg, Freyr, Navlin)
data_reliability: high — primary regulatory document (Resolution 2025010547) plus U.S. government commercial service confirmation
